source: Invest Like the Best — Investing through a bear market (EP.167)
summary: Days-of-solvency-at-zero-revenue is the new critical liquidity metric replacing Altman Z-score and debt/EBITDA
summary_extended: Baker argues that the COVID-19 bear market has rendered traditional credit metrics like the Altman Z-score, debt/EBITDA ratios, and interest coverage ratios obsolete, replacing them with a new emergency liquidity framework: 'days of solvency at zero revenue.' He contends that every serious investment firm is now running this analysis — asking how many days a company can survive if revenues drop to zero — because the pandemic has created a scenario where revenue can disappear entirely and instantaneously. Baker emphasizes that cash on hand dominates all other considerations, and that the only covenant structures that truly matter in this environment are those tied to revolving credit facilities, since those are the instruments companies are drawing on for emergency liquidity. The investment implication is that portfolio managers must screen holdings and prospective investments through this new lens, prioritizing companies with high cash balances and low fixed-cost burn rates, and being especially cautious about companies with tight revolver covenants. No specific companies or sectors are named; this is a framework-level thesis applicable across all equity and credit analysis.
detail: Baker argues every investment firm is now running a 'days of solvency with zero revenue' analysis — a metric that had never previously been run. This supersedes traditional metrics like Altman Z-score, debt/EBITDA, and interest coverage. Cash dominates covenants; revolver covenants are the only place covenants truly matter.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a definitive framework shift he is actively applying, with strong conviction language and a clear rationale.
quote_fragment: days of solvency with zero revenue is the equation being run everywhere
